segments.text: Hi, this is Brian Cipollone with the
solutions engineering team here at
Sonar. In this segment, I'll be walking
you through some of the exciting
capabilities we've recently added to
SonarQube. We've been incredibly busy
adding new functionality that will help
our users verify their applications. Now
more than ever, it's critical that every
line of code is subject to a thorough
verification process. As AI writes more
code, some users may never have written
any of the code in the code bases
they're reviewing. We want to make sure
that Sonar continues to be your trusted
advisor as we enter the age of AI
powered SDLC. So let's jump in and take
a look at the latest from Sonar.
Software composition analysis or SCA is
a vital tool for managing risks
associated with open-source software
dependencies. With SonarQube's SCA
capability, we help you automatically
identify and track the open-source
libraries used in your projects,
scanning them for known security
vulnerabilities or CVEs, license
compliance issues in malicious packages.
This ensures that AI generated code or
any code that you integrate is built on
a secure and compliant foundation.
Dependency risks are detected alongside
existing SonarQube analysis and are
presented to users with easy to follow
guidance on the nature of the issue,
where the issue was found, and
instructions on how to fix it. Sonar
also partners with the open source
maintainer community to gather
firstparty insights on the
exploitability of these vulnerabilities
so you can prioritize remediation. We
also provide tools to help users manage
exceptions and a full audit trail of
these decisions. These tools are
integrated with SonarQube for IDE to help
our users address these dependencies
earlier in their process. All
dependencies are centrally managed and
you can use the SBOM export feature to
download your software bill of materials
in popular formats. SBOMs are exportable
from both the project and portfolio
level. Portfolio reporting capabilities
include a comprehensive overview of your
dependency risks across multiple
projects. This allows management and
security teams to quickly assess the
overall exposure, enabling a strategic
top-down view for prioritizing
mitigation efforts and ensuring AI
generated code is integrated responsibly
across the entire organization. License
profile management allows you to define
and enforce organizational policies
governing the use of open source
licenses. This feature automatically
identifies the licenses associated with
all of your project dependencies,
flagging any that conflict with your
established policies. This proactive
management capability is critical for
maintaining legal compliance, especially
when integrating AI generated code,
ensuring your projects remain free from
intellectual property risks. To
effectively enforce your open source
policies, SonarQube integrates
dependency risk directly into the
quality gate. This means that detection
of license violations or high-risk
dependencies can be set to break builds
or block merges. The quality gate can
prevent AI generated code or any new
changes from being merged or deployed
until all critical dependency issues are
remediated. Software composition
analysis is now available as part of our
advanced security add-on for SonarQube
Server and SonarQube Cloud. The SonarQube
MCP server represents a significant
architectural evolution designed to
bring SonarQube analysis to the modern
development landscape which is
increasingly defined by AI assistant
coding and agentic workflows. The MCP
server provides a unified AI integration
allowing SonarQube's analysis and
reporting capabilities to be quickly
adapted to IDEs like Cursor and
Windsurf as well as agentic workflows
like those enabled by Claude Code. The
example shown here shows an AI coding
agent being asked to remediate some
legacy technical debt. The agent starts
out by contacting SonarQube via the MCP
server. The MCP server instantly returns
a concise structured response with the
location of the most critical issues.
With this contextual feedback, the exact
location of issues, the nature of the
findings, and suggestions for
remediation, the agent immediately
refactors the code to eliminate issues.
This interaction demonstrates the power
of connecting AI coding agents to
SonarQube. The MCP server turns SonarQube's
deep code analysis and
orchestration capabilities into
actionable services for AI agents,
allowing them to autonomously write code
that is fundamentally more secure,
reliable, and maintainable.
These improvements are baked in the very
first commit and can save countless
hours of review. The SonarQube MCP
server is free to use and available via
GitHub for all versions of SonarQube
Server and SonarQube Cloud. Sonar's
commitment to continuously expanding and
refining our language support is driven
by a singular goal to ensure that
quality and security are non-negotiable
standards no matter what technology your
developers or AI agents are using. As
the ecosystem evolves with new languages
and frameworks and as AI generated code
increasingly becomes a part of every
codebase, SonarQube must evolve with
it. Our improvements ensure
comprehensive coverage, deep analysis,
and relevant feedback across your entire
software portfolio, making sure every
line of code, whether it be human or AI
written, meet your standards. We're
excited to introduce full-fledged
analysis for Rust, a language we see
rapidly gaining traction for its
performance and safety guarantees. We've
also added comprehensive support for
CI/CD automation with GitHub Actions and
general scripting with shell scripts.
Finally, to ensure your infrastructure
and application settings are secure from
the start, we now analyze common
configuration files like YAML and
properties files. In addition to
quality, security, and reliability
analysis, secrets detection is embedded
in all of our new code analyzers. Our
commitment to code quality goes far
beyond introducing new languages. We're
continuously enhancing our analysis for
the languages you already use. This is
vital as AI generated code increasingly
targets these established ecosystems.
For Java, we've introduced new rules
focused on advanced security
vulnerabilities and modern language
features. In Python, our analysis now
provides deeper insights into
performance bottlenecks in framework
specific issues. And for .NET, we've added
comprehensive support for newer versions
alongside more granular bug detection.
Furthermore, we've delivered a massive
expansion in support for several key
languages for Go, Ruby, and Apex. We've
dramatically increased the breadth of
our coverage, including the critical
expansion of security analysis. Those
are just the highlights. Overall, we've
added a thousand new rules across 31
languages in the past year and will
continue to add more. These rules become
immediately available on SonarQube Cloud
and are released alongside new versions
of SonarQube Server. While we're on the
subject of rules, I wanted to share that
Sonar has continued to expand our
secrets detection capabilities. What
sets Sonar apart is the depth and
precision of our engine. We've
introduced over 340 rules to detect more
than 450 secrets patterns across
hundreds of cloud services and APIs.
Because we combine regular expressions
with semantic analysis, we can maintain
a remarkably low false positive rate.
This means AI coding agents and
developers get high signal actionable
feedback without alert fatigue. By
stopping secrets before a commit,
SonarQube helps you build trust into
every line of code. Secret detection is
available on all versions of SonarQube
Cloud, SonarQube Server, and on the
desktop with SonarQube for IDE and
command line tools. This year, we've
made a number of improvements to deliver
a more intelligent and accessible user
experience designed for both developers
and executives. We're working to address
one of the biggest pain points for
organizations adopting AI coding agents,
strategic visibility. To do this, we're
introducing customizable reporting that
allows our users to monitor high level code
health and regulatory compliance across
your entire organization at a glance.
We've rearchitected the interface with a
modernized workspace to provide the data
you need exactly where you need it.
Custom dashboards are a powerful way to
track the health of your code. Tracking
key indicators and trends is vital, but
every team has different priorities.
This feature allows you to move beyond
the one-size-fits-all metrics and to
build views that fit your specific
needs. When you first open the new
dashboards tab, you'll find the project
health dashboard. This is a built-in
view that covers the essentials:
security, reliability, and
maintainability. It's a great starting
point because it allows you to drill
down into the details behind any
specific metric to understand exactly
what's happening in your codebase. But
the real power lies in the ability to
create a custom dashboard from scratch.
Once you give it a name and description,
you enter edit mode, which gives you a
blank canvas to add widgets and
sections. For example, if you want to
see your issue count and how it's
trending, you can add a count widget
with a trend indicator. You can even
filter these widgets to show data for
new code versus overall code, giving you
an immediate sense of your current
sprint's quality. You can also use line
charts to visualize data over time or
donut charts to break down issues by
severity or programming language. To
keep things organized, the tool allows
you to create specific sections. You
might create one section dedicated
entirely to security, grouping together
security issue counts, trends, and
severity distributions so you can
understand your security posture at a
single glance. Once you're happy with
the layout, just save your changes.
These dashboards are designed to be
dynamic and Sonar is constantly adding
new widgets to the library. Whether
you're a developer tracking your own
progress or a lead monitoring multiple
indicators, these custom dashboards
provide you with the visibility you need
to keep your projects on track. Custom
dashboards are available on SonarQube Cloud
with an enterprise plan subscription. We
understand that reporting is not just
about tracking internal health. It's
about demonstrating compliance and
security posture to stakeholders,
auditors, and regulators. We
significantly upgraded this reporting
suite to provide the strategic
visibility you need. To provide a
top-down view of security, we're
introducing portfolio security reports.
This allows security and management
teams to get a single consolidated view
of security hotspots and vulnerabilities
across all the projects within a defined
portfolio.
This is vital for proactively managing
risks, especially with the velocity of
AI generated code. These reports are now
available as downloadable PDF reports,
making it effortless to share snapshots
of your compliance status. Finally,
we're adding dedicated security
reporting to help organizations meet
common compliance mandates. We now offer
out of the box reporting for standards
like STIG and Casa, ensuring that
SonarQube serves as a source for
critical security and compliance
evidence.
In today's fast-paced development
landscape, data is only valuable if it's
where you need it, when you need it. At
Sonar, we're bringing the powerful
insights of SonarQube directly into your
existing development tools. We're doing
this by building new and deeper
integrations with platforms like Slack
for immediate alerts, Jira for
streamline issue tracking, and with JFrog
to provide evidence of your code's
quality. Our goal is simple, to make
code quality and security an effortless
part of your daily workflow, no matter
where your developers or AI agents are
working. Now, let's walk through two of
these integrations. The Jira integration
is designed to bridge the gap between
code quality and your project management
workflow. By connecting SonarQube
projects to your Jira projects, you can
bring critical code insights directly
into the tools your team uses every day
to track work. The core functionality
allows you to push SonarQube issues
such as bugs, vulnerabilities, and code
smells into actionable Jira work items.
This creates a single source of truth
for all tasks, eliminating the need to
manually recreate issues. When an issue
is pushed to Jira, the resulting work
item includes all the essential context,
a clear description of the problem in
exact location in code. This streamlined
workflow ensures that developers can
prioritize and fix code quality issues
as part of their regular sprints,
improving accountability and
significantly shortening the feedback
loop between finding an issue and
resolving it. Our Slack integration is
simple yet powerful. Teams can subscribe
to their SonarQube projects and get
notifications anytime their quality gate
status changes. These notifications will
help teams respond to issues quickly and
efficiently. Slack integration is
currently available on SonarQube Cloud
and SonarQube Server. These are just two
of the dozens of integrations we've
recently built along with our partners.
You can find a complete list at
sonarsource.com.
As organizations scale their use of
SonarQube, especially to manage the
velocity and scope of AI generated code,
they require enterprise-grade controls
for security and resource management.
I'm going to walk through a couple of
these new features in SonarQube Cloud
specifically designed to meet these
needs, ensuring centralized control and
governance across your entire
organization. One of the most critical
additions is scoped organizational
tokens. Previously, users often relied
on personal tokens, which while
functional, presented a security risk if
the user left the organization or the
token was compromised. With scoped
organizational tokens, administrators
can create tokens that are tied to the
organization, not to individual users.
These tokens are highly configurable,
granting access to specific projects
within the organization. This capability
significantly enhances security and
compliance by allowing secure fine grain
access for your CI/CD pipelines in AI
agent workflows, all without relying on
a single individual's credentials. To
further strengthen your security, we've
introduced IP allow lists. This feature
lets administrators restrict access to
SonarQube Cloud resources only to approved
trusted IP addresses. This is a
fundamental security control that
prevents unauthorized external access,
adding another layer of protection for
your code analysis data. To help
administrators manage their SonarQube Cloud
enterprise plan licenses, we're
introducing enterprise level line of
code allocation. This gives
administrators a centralized top-down
view of line of code consumption across
the entire enterprise, allowing for
proactive allocation of resources to
different teams and projects as their
needs and the volume of AI generated
code fluctuates. Thank you so much for
joining me on this walkthrough of our
latest features. If you aren't already a
SonarQube user, you can sign up for a
free SonarQube Cloud account at
sonarcloud.io.
